title: The Hidden Cost of Failed Payments in Early-Stage SaaS
meta_description: Failed payments quietly eat 5-8% of MRR at most bootstrapped SaaS. Here's what actually works to recover them — and which tools are overpriced.
h1: The Hidden Cost of Failed Payments in Early-Stage SaaS
body_markdown:
A founder I know at $14k MRR lost $1,100 in March to failed payments and didn't notice for three weeks. He found out because one of the churned customers emailed asking why his login stopped working. The customer wanted to stay. The card had expired in February. Stripe had retried four times, given up, and cancelled the subscription silently — exactly as configured.

That's the thing nobody tells you about failed payments SaaS economics: most of your involuntary churn isn't customers leaving. It's customers whose banks issued new cards and who never got a useful email about it.

I've now tested five tools in the dunning and revenue recovery space over the last two years — Baremetrics Recover, Churn Buster, Stunning, Chargebee's retry logic, and a homemade Zapier thing I'm embarrassed about. I have opinions. Some of these are good. Some charge $99/mo to send three emails you could write yourself in an afternoon.

## The actual math on failed payments

Stripe's own data (they published this in a 2022 Billing report) puts involuntary churn at roughly 9% of attempted charges for subscription businesses. Profitwell has quoted similar numbers — somewhere between 5% and 8% of MRR lost to card failures annually for the average SaaS. At $10k MRR, that's $500-$800 a month you're leaving on the table. At $30k MRR, $1,500-$2,400.

And here's where it gets worse. Stripe's default Smart Retries recover maybe 40-50% of failed charges if you leave everything on autopilot. The other half? Silently cancelled after the retry window expires, usually 3-4 weeks later, often without the customer having any idea.

So your real exposure is something like 3-4% of MRR permanently lost, every year, from people who never meant to leave.

I got this wrong for the first eight months of running my own thing. I assumed Stripe was handling it because the dashboard said "Smart Retries: On." It was handling the easy cases. The hard cases — expired cards, insufficient funds on a specific day of the month, 3DS challenges on European cards — were just disappearing.

## Why Stripe's defaults aren't enough

Stripe's retry logic is fine for what it is. It's a generic system that works across millions of businesses. But it has three gaps that matter for small SaaS:

The emails it sends are Stripe-branded, which 40% of your customers will mark as phishing or ignore. The retry schedule doesn't know when your customer's payday is (most card failures for B2C-adjacent SaaS are just timing — the card works on the 3rd, not the 28th). And if a customer needs to update their card, the default Stripe hosted update page is functional but ugly, and there's no way to A/B test anything.

There's a well-known HN thread from 2021 titled something like "Show HN: I recovered $40k in failed Stripe payments with a 3-email sequence" where the founder explained he just wrote three plain-text emails from his own domain and recovered 3x what Stripe's auto-emails did. That's the benchmark. Plain-text, from a real person, with a direct update link.

Most tools in this space are trying to sell you back something you could build in a weekend. Some of them are still worth the money. Others aren't.

## The tools I've tested, ranked honestly

Let me just say what I think.

**Churn Buster** is the best of the dedicated recovery tools. They charge based on recovered revenue (around 20% last I checked, with a floor) which aligns incentives properly. Their email sequences are good. The team clearly understands the problem. If you're above $20k MRR and you don't want to think about this, use them.

**Baremetrics Recover** is bundled with Baremetrics' analytics at the Startup plan ($129/mo) or higher. It's fine. The emails are generic. The dashboard is pretty. If you're already paying for Baremetrics for analytics — which I think is overpriced for under $20k MRR — then turning on Recover is free-ish. If you're not, don't buy Baremetrics just for Recover.

**Stunning** felt dated when I trialed it in 2023. The UI looks like 2014. I'm sure it works. I didn't keep it.

**Chargebee's built-in dunning** is solid but you have to already be on Chargebee, which is its own huge decision and probably overkill below $50k MRR.

**My Zapier contraption** recovered some money and also sent one customer four emails in 20 minutes because I messed up a filter. Don't do this unless you enjoy explaining yourself to angry CTOs on a Sunday.

The ranking if you want it in one line: Churn Buster > Chargebee > Baremetrics Recover > Stunning > homemade.

## What actually moves the needle

Here's what I've seen work, across maybe a dozen founders I've compared notes with:

1. Send the first retry email from a real human address at your own domain, not Stripe. Response rates roughly double. This is free.
2. Retry on days 1, 3, 5, 7, and 14 — not Stripe's default cadence. The day-14 retry catches the "paid monthly, card declined because of timing" crowd.
3. Include the last 4 digits of the card that failed. People have multiple cards. They want to know which one.
4. Never, ever downgrade or suspend access on the first failure. Give them two weeks minimum. The cost of one angry-but-loyal customer losing access for 48 hours is worse than the cost of two weeks of free service for someone who was going to pay anyway.
5. Actually read the failure codes. `insufficient_funds` needs a different email than `expired_card` needs a different email than `do_not_honor`. Stripe returns these in the webhook. Most tools ignore them.

That last one is where most of the dedicated tools fall short, by the way. They treat every failure the same. A customer whose card expired needs a 30-second update flow. A customer whose card got declined for fraud needs reassurance that you're not trying to charge them twice.

## The part nobody writes about: knowing it happened

The failed payment is only half the problem. The other half is that most founders don't find out about it until weeks later when they're reconciling numbers, or when a customer emails, or when they finally sit down to look at their actual MRR instead of the vanity number Stripe shows on the dashboard.

Stripe's dashboard shows you gross volume. It does not, in any useful way, show you net MRR after failed payments, refunds, and involuntary churn. You have to build that yourself or pay someone to build it for you.

> If you're checking Stripe's "MRR" number on the homepage, you're not looking at your MRR. You're looking at a number Stripe made up that vaguely correlates with it.

This is why I built — and why a few other people have built — tools that sit on top of the Stripe API and show you the actual number. One of them is ours: [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) reads your Stripe data and surfaces failed payments, at-risk MRR, and real net MRR without making you log into Stripe every morning. It's aimed at the $1k-$50k MRR range where Baremetrics is overkill and a spreadsheet is underkill. That's the pitch. Use it or don't — but if you take one thing from this post, it should be that you need *some* view of this data that isn't the Stripe dashboard.

## What I'd do if I were starting today at $3k MRR

Don't pay for a recovery tool yet. At $3k MRR, even a great tool will recover maybe $80-$120/mo for you, and most of them cost more than that or take a percentage floor.

Instead: write three emails. First one goes out the day a charge fails, from your personal address, short, with the card's last 4 digits and an update link. Second one three days later. Third one at day 10, slightly firmer. You can set this up with Stripe webhooks and Postmark in about four hours.

Above $15k MRR, the math flips. Your time is worth more than the tool costs, and a dedicated service will recover 10-15% more than your homemade setup because they've tested the copy across thousands of accounts. That's when I'd move to Churn Buster.

And somewhere in between — $5k to $15k — you're in the awkward middle where the homemade version is good enough but you keep meaning to upgrade it and never do. That's where the money actually leaks. Not because the tools don't exist, but because you're too busy shipping features to care about a problem that compounds quietly at 5% a year.

Which is, when you think about it, exactly the kind of problem early-stage founders are worst at solving. It doesn't scream. It just drips.
cover_image_prompt: Abstract editorial illustration, dark mode, deep navy and charcoal background. A minimal geometric representation of a credit card slowly dissolving into small particles that drift downward like sand through an hourglass, with faint teal and muted coral accents catching the light. Subtle grid lines suggest a financial dashboard in the deep background, heavily blurred. Concept-art style, premium tech magazine aesthetic, painterly shadows, negative space dominant. Mood: quiet financial erosion, invisible leakage. No text, no people, no logos, no literal dollar signs.
